title: Economics as a social science and the role of models
summary: Economics studies human behaviour, so it cannot run the controlled experiments open to the natural sciences. Instead economists build simplified models, hold other factors constant with ceteris paribus, assume rational decision-making, and test their positive claims against evidence. This lesson explains the scientific method in economics, the role and limits of models, the positive/normative distinction, refutation, and the behavioural critique that questions the rationality assumption.
learningObjectives: Explain why economics is a social science and how it applies the scientific method to human behaviour.
Explain the role of models, assumptions, ceteris paribus and rational economic decision-making, and evaluate their limitations.
Distinguish clearly between positive and normative statements and explain the role of refutation in positive economics.
Outline the behavioural critique of the rational decision-maker and what it means for the usefulness of models.
levelNote: Content is identical at SL and HL; HL extension material begins in Unit 2.

simpleExplanation.title: Economics: Mapping Human Choices
simpleExplanation.summary: Economics is a social science: it studies people, not particles, so it cannot run clean laboratory experiments. To make sense of a complex world it builds simplified models, holds other things constant, and tests its factual claims against evidence.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of a London Underground map. It leaves out streets, distances and scale, yet it is brilliant for the one job it is built for — planning a journey. An economic model is the same: it deliberately strips away detail so you can see one relationship clearly. Calling a model 'wrong' because it simplifies is like calling the tube map wrong because it isn't drawn to scale.
steps: Observe a real problem (e.g. why did rents rise?) and build a simplified model of it.
Make assumptions — above all ceteris paribus (all other things equal) and rational decision-making — to isolate one relationship.
Turn the model into a positive, testable prediction ('if interest rates fall, quantity of housing demanded rises').
Test the prediction against evidence; if it is refuted, revise or discard the model. Keep the positive analysis separate from normative 'ought' judgements.

flashcards.back: A discipline that studies human society and behaviour using systematic, evidence-based methods. Economics is a social science because it studies how people allocate scarce resources.

Observe → form a hypothesis → deduce a testable (positive) prediction → gather evidence → confirm or refute. Because economists rarely run controlled experiments, testing relies mainly on real-world and statistical data.
A deliberately simplified representation of reality that captures the key variables and relationships in an economic situation. Examples: the demand and supply model, the PPC.

Simplifying conditions a model takes as given so it can isolate one relationship. Good assumptions are unrealistic in detail but useful — they make the model tractable without destroying its predictive power.
Latin for 'all other things being equal'. The core modelling assumption that lets economists examine the effect of one variable at a time by holding every other influence constant.

The standard assumption that decision-makers have clear goals and choose the option that best achieves them: consumers maximise utility (satisfaction) and firms maximise profit, given their constraints.
An objective claim about what is, was, or will be, that can be tested against evidence and shown true or false. Example: 'A rise in the minimum wage raises youth unemployment.'

A subjective value judgement about what ought to be. It cannot be proven true or false by evidence. Example: 'The government should raise the minimum wage to reduce poverty.' Signalled by words like should, ought, fair, too high.

The idea that a genuine positive claim must be capable of being proven false by evidence. A theory that no observation could ever contradict is not scientific. Repeatedly surviving attempts at refutation strengthens a theory but never proves it beyond doubt.

Evidence from behavioural economics that people are only 'boundedly rational' — using rules of thumb, biases and habits — so the strict rationality assumption is often violated. This limits, but does not destroy, the usefulness of standard models.
A positive statement is a factual claim, not a favourable one. 'A recession will cut incomes by 10%' is positive but describes a bad outcome. Positive = testable; it says nothing about desirability.

flashcards.front: Common Trap: a model is 'wrong' because it is unrealistic
flashcards.back: Models are meant to simplify. They are judged by whether their predictions are useful and survive testing, not by whether every assumption is literally true.

quickCheck.prompt: Which of these is a NORMATIVE statement?
options: Unemployment rose to 6% last quarter.
A carbon tax will raise the price of petrol.
The government ought to tax carbon more heavily.
Higher interest rates tend to reduce borrowing.
quickCheck.answer: The government ought to tax carbon more heavily.

quickCheck.prompt: Why is the statement 'A rise in the price of coffee will reduce the quantity demanded' classed as POSITIVE?
options: Because it describes a good outcome for consumers
Because it is a value judgement about fairness
Because it can be tested against evidence and refuted
Because it uses the word 'reduce'
quickCheck.answer: Because it can be tested against evidence and refuted

quickCheck.prompt: The ceteris paribus assumption is used in economic models mainly to:
options: Prove that a model is completely realistic
Isolate the effect of one variable by holding others constant
Turn a normative statement into a positive one
Guarantee that predictions are always correct
quickCheck.answer: Isolate the effect of one variable by holding others constant

quickCheck.prompt: A student writes: 'The demand and supply model must be wrong because it ignores advertising and habits.' The best economist's response is:
options: Agree — any model that leaves out detail is invalid
A model is judged by the usefulness of its predictions, not by whether it includes every detail
The model should be discarded and replaced by a list of all real-world factors
Ceteris paribus makes the model impossible to test
quickCheck.answer: A model is judged by the usefulness of its predictions, not by whether it includes every detail

quickCheck.prompt: The behavioural critique of the standard rationality assumption argues that people:
options: Always maximise utility with perfect information
Never respond to prices or incentives at all
Often rely on rules of thumb and biases, so are only boundedly rational
Behave exactly like particles in physics
quickCheck.answer: Often rely on rules of thumb and biases, so are only boundedly rational

faq.q: If economic models are so simplified, are they actually useful?
faq.a: Yes — usefulness is the whole point. Like a tube map that ignores scale and streets, a model deliberately strips away detail so one relationship becomes visible. Its value is measured by whether its positive predictions are helpful and survive testing, not by whether every assumption is literally true. A perfectly realistic 'model' would be as complex as reality itself and therefore useless.

faq.q: Can a statement be both positive and normative?
faq.a: A single statement is one or the other, but a paragraph often contains both. Typically a normative conclusion ('we should do X') is backed by positive evidence ('because the data show Y will happen'). The examinable skill is separating the factual claims, which evidence can settle, from the value judgements, which it cannot.

faq.q: Does the behavioural critique mean the standard model is useless?
faq.a: No. Behavioural economics shows that the strict rationality assumption is frequently broken — people use shortcuts, are influenced by how choices are framed, and value fairness. This limits how far standard models can predict behaviour and refines them, but the core insight that people respond to incentives and prices still holds and remains a powerful modelling tool.

faq.q: Why must a positive statement be refutable to be scientific?
faq.a: Because a claim that no possible observation could ever contradict tells us nothing — it can never be tested. Refutability means we can specify what evidence would prove the claim false. A theory earns credibility by repeatedly surviving genuine attempts to refute it, though surviving is never the same as being proven true forever.

sections.content: This lesson is about how economists think and work. Economics is a social science: it studies human behaviour, which cannot be locked in a laboratory and controlled the way a chemist controls a reaction. We will see how economists get around this with simplified models and assumptions — above all ceteris paribus and rational decision-making — how they test their factual claims by trying to refute them, why the positive/normative distinction matters, and how behavioural economics has recently challenged one of the discipline's oldest assumptions.
Economics as a social science

Economics is a social science, not a natural science. Natural sciences such as physics and chemistry study the physical world, where researchers can usually run controlled experiments — changing one variable while holding everything else fixed — to establish reliable laws. Economics instead studies the choices of people: individuals, firms and governments deciding how to use scarce resources. Its subject matter is human behaviour, and that changes everything about its method.

The central difficulty is that you cannot run a clean controlled experiment on a whole economy. You cannot find two identical countries, cut taxes in one, and hold every other influence constant to measure the pure effect. People also have free will and expectations, so they may react to a policy in ways that undermine the very prediction being tested. Because of this, economists lean on models and on the statistical analysis of real-world and historical data rather than on laboratory experiments.

items: Economics studies human behaviour under scarcity, which makes it a social science.
Controlled experiments are usually impossible, unethical or impractical for a whole economy, so economists rely on models and data.
Economic 'laws' are tendencies that hold ceteris paribus, not the unbreakable certainties of the natural sciences.

sections.content: The scientific method in economics

Despite these limits, economics still applies the scientific method. An economist observes a phenomenon, forms a hypothesis about the relationship between variables, deduces a testable prediction from it, and then confronts that prediction with evidence. If the evidence contradicts the prediction, the hypothesis is revised or rejected; if it survives, confidence in the theory grows. The difference from the natural sciences is not the logic of the method but the testing ground: mostly observational data rather than controlled experiments.

items: Observe a real-world pattern or problem.
Form a hypothesis and build a model of the relationship.
Deduce a positive, testable prediction (holding other things constant).
Gather evidence and try to refute the prediction — then confirm, revise or discard the theory.

sections.content: The role of economic models, assumptions and ceteris paribus

Because the real world is overwhelmingly complex, economists use models: deliberately simplified representations of reality that capture the key variables and the relationships between them. A model such as demand and supply, or the production possibilities curve, throws away thousands of real details in order to make one mechanism visible. The skill of good modelling lies in choosing which details to ignore — a model is meant to be simpler than reality, not a copy of it.

Models are built on assumptions — conditions taken as given so the analysis stays manageable. The single most important assumption is ceteris paribus, Latin for 'all other things being equal'. It lets the economist change one variable and trace its effect while holding every other influence constant. For example, to study how the price of a good affects the quantity demanded, we assume consumer incomes, tastes and the prices of related goods do not change at the same time.
Conceptually: Quantity demanded ($Q_d$) = f(Price | ceteris paribus)

Assumptions are not a weakness to apologise for — they are what makes a model work. The test of an assumption is not whether it is literally true but whether the model built on it yields useful, testable predictions. Complaining that a model 'ignores' something real usually misses the point: it ignores that thing on purpose, precisely to isolate what it is trying to explain.

When you explain any diagram — demand and supply, the PPC, cost curves — state the ceteris paribus assumption explicitly, e.g. 'assuming all other factors are held constant, a rise in price reduces the quantity demanded'. Examiners read this as evidence that you understand economic methodology, not just the diagram.
Rational economic decision-making

Standard economic models also assume rational economic decision-making: that people have clear objectives and choose the option that best achieves them, given their constraints. Consumers are assumed to maximise utility (satisfaction) from their limited income, and firms to maximise profit. This assumption is powerful because it makes behaviour predictable: if we know what people are trying to achieve, we can predict how they will respond when prices, incomes or incentives change.

sections.question:
Read the passage and identify ONE positive statement and ONE normative statement, justifying each choice.

'The government raised the tax on sugary drinks by 20% last year. Sales of these drinks fell by about 8% over the following twelve months. This is a sensible policy, because it is wrong for firms to profit from products that harm children's health. The tax should be increased again next year.'
sections.solution:
**Positive statement:** 'Sales of these drinks fell by about 8% over the following twelve months.'

**Justification:** This is a factual claim about what happened. It can be tested against real sales data and could be shown to be true or false — it is therefore refutable. It reports an outcome without judging whether that outcome is good or bad, which is the hallmark of a positive statement. [2]

**Normative statement:** 'This is a sensible policy, because it is wrong for firms to profit from products that harm children's health.' (Accept also: 'The tax should be increased again next year.')

**Justification:** This rests on a value judgement — the words 'sensible' and 'wrong', and 'should', express an opinion about what ought to be done. No amount of data can prove or disprove that the policy is 'wrong' or that the tax 'should' rise; reasonable people can disagree. It is prescriptive, not descriptive, so it is normative. [2]

sections.content: Positive versus normative statements

A defining feature of thinking like an economist is separating positive from normative statements. Positive statements are objective and descriptive: they concern what is, was, or will be, and can be tested against evidence — 'the UK inflation rate was 9% in 2022'. Normative statements are subjective and prescriptive: they concern what ought to be and rest on value judgements that evidence cannot settle — 'the government ought to do more to control inflation'. Words such as should, ought, fair, unfair, too high and better usually flag a normative claim.

items: **Positive** — objective, evidence-based, testable, about 'what is'. Can be shown true or false.
**Normative** — subjective, value-laden, about 'what should be'. Cannot be proven true or false.
Keeping them apart matters: good policy debate first agrees the positive consequences, then argues the normative desirability. Mixing them lets opinions masquerade as facts.

sections.content: Refutation and the limits of proof

Positive economics rests on the idea of refutation. For a claim to be scientific, it must be possible to state what evidence would prove it false. A theory that no conceivable observation could contradict is empty — it explains nothing because it rules nothing out. Economists gain confidence in a theory when it repeatedly survives serious attempts to refute it, but surviving is never final proof: a future observation could still overturn it. This is why economic knowledge is always provisional and open to revision.
The limits of models and the behavioural critique

Models are indispensable but limited. Two limits stand out. First, ceteris paribus rarely holds in reality — several things usually change at once, so a model's prediction can be thrown off by a simultaneous shift the model held constant. Second, and more fundamentally, the rationality assumption is often violated. Behavioural economics has gathered strong evidence that people are only 'boundedly rational': they rely on rules of thumb, are swayed by how choices are framed, weigh losses more heavily than equivalent gains, and often care about fairness rather than pure self-interest.

The right conclusion is not that standard models should be thrown away. It is that they should be used with awareness of their limits and refined where the evidence demands it. The core insight — that people respond to incentives and prices — remains robust and useful. Behavioural economics sharpens the standard toolkit rather than replacing it, which is exactly how a healthy science absorbs refutation: by revising, not abandoning, its models.

sections.question: An economist uses a simple demand and supply model to predict that a government subsidy on solar panels will lower their price and raise the quantity sold. (a) Explain the role of the ceteris paribus assumption in this prediction. (b) Explain ONE limitation of relying on this model.
sections.solution:
**(a) Role of ceteris paribus:** The prediction isolates the effect of the subsidy by assuming all other influences on the solar-panel market are held constant — consumer incomes, the price of grid electricity (a substitute), production technology and preferences for green energy. Holding these fixed, the subsidy lowers producers' costs, shifts supply to the right, and the model can attribute the resulting fall in price and rise in quantity purely to the subsidy. Without ceteris paribus we could not separate the subsidy's effect from everything else. [2]

**(b) One limitation:** In reality ceteris paribus does not hold. Suppose that, in the same year, a technological breakthrough sharply cuts production costs and rising fossil-fuel prices make solar more attractive. Both would also shift the curves, so the actual price and quantity would differ from the model's simple prediction. The model simplifies a changing world and can therefore mislead if several factors move at once — a reason to treat its prediction as a tendency, not a precise forecast. [2]

sections.content: Paper 1 part (b) questions ask you to 'discuss' or 'evaluate'. That is your cue to combine both types of analysis: first use models and theory to explain the likely outcomes (positive), then judge those outcomes from different stakeholders' perspectives (normative). Signposting which is which — 'the evidence suggests… whereas whether this is desirable depends on…' — is exactly the discipline this lesson trains.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Treating 'positive' as 'good'** — a positive statement is testable, not favourable. 'Incomes will fall 10%' is positive but describes a bad outcome.
**Mislabelling a value judgement as positive** — if a statement uses should, ought, fair or too high, or cannot be settled by evidence, it is normative, however factual it sounds.
**Calling a model 'wrong' because it is unrealistic** — models simplify on purpose; they are judged by the usefulness of their predictions, not by whether every assumption is literally true.
**Forgetting ceteris paribus** — omitting the assumption when explaining a diagram loses methodology marks and invites the objection 'but other things changed too'.
**Claiming a theory is 'proven'** — positive economics can refute or support a theory, never prove it beyond all doubt; knowledge stays provisional.
**Overstating the behavioural critique** — bounded rationality limits and refines standard models; it does not show that people ignore incentives altogether.

sections.content: Model answer — marked against the real IB markband

sections.question: Paper 1, part (a): Explain, using examples, the distinction between positive and normative statements and the role of ceteris paribus in economic models. [10 marks]
sections.solution:
**Model answer:**
Economics is a social science that studies human behaviour, and much of it works by building simplified models. Two ideas are central to how economists reason within those models: the positive/normative distinction and the ceteris paribus assumption.

A POSITIVE statement is an objective, factual claim about what is, was or will be. It can be tested against evidence and shown to be true or false — that is, it is refutable. For example, 'a 20% tax on sugary drinks reduced their sales by 8%' is positive, because sales data can confirm or contradict it. A NORMATIVE statement is a subjective value judgement about what ought to be. It rests on opinion and cannot be proven true or false by evidence. For example, 'the government should tax sugary drinks more heavily' is normative — the word 'should' signals a value judgement about which reasonable people may disagree. Note that 'positive' does not mean 'good': a positive statement such as 'unemployment will rise to 8%' is factual but describes an undesirable outcome. Separating the two matters because it keeps factual analysis distinct from opinion, so that policy debate can first establish the likely consequences (positive) before arguing over whether they are desirable (normative).

Ceteris paribus, meaning 'all other things being equal', is the assumption that lets economists build and test positive claims in the first place. Because a whole economy cannot be put in a controlled experiment, the economist instead holds every other influence constant and changes one variable at a time. For instance, the positive claim 'a rise in the price of coffee reduces the quantity of coffee demanded' only holds ceteris paribus — assuming incomes, tastes and the prices of substitutes such as tea are unchanged. This assumption isolates the price–quantity relationship so it can be modelled and tested; without it we could never tell which factor caused an observed change. Its limitation is that in reality other things rarely stay equal, so a prediction can be thrown off when several factors move at once — which is why economists treat such predictions as tendencies rather than certainties.

Together, these ideas show how economics functions as a social science: ceteris paribus makes it possible to state precise positive claims that can be refuted by evidence, while the positive/normative distinction keeps that evidence-based analysis separate from the value judgements that guide policy.

---
**How this maps to our IB markband (Paper 1 part (a), out of 10):**
The top band (9–10) requires that the demands of the question are understood and addressed, that relevant economic theory is *FULLY explained*, and that relevant terms are *used appropriately THROUGHOUT* (with a relevant diagram fully explained *where appropriate* — no diagram is needed for a purely conceptual question like this one).
- Addresses every demand: the positive/normative distinction AND the role of ceteris paribus are both explained, not just one.
- Theory fully explained: each concept is defined, illustrated with an apt example, and its purpose and limits are drawn out — refutability for positive statements, isolation of one variable for ceteris paribus.
- Terms used appropriately throughout: positive, normative, value judgement, refutable, ceteris paribus, quantity demanded, substitutes all appear correctly and in context.
- The 'positive ≠ good' clarification and the ceteris-paribus limitation show understanding rather than mere recall.
An answer that only DEFINED positive and normative with one example, or described ceteris paribus without explaining why it is needed, would sit in the 3–4 band ('theory described') or 5–6 band ('theory partly explained') — not the top. The move that reaches 9–10 is EXPLAINING each concept's purpose and limits with relevant examples, which is exactly what our marking engine rewards.

sections.content: This is precisely what the Practice button does: write your own answer to this question, submit it, and our marking engine grades it against this same official markband — telling you which band you reached and why. The habit that moves you up the bands here: give each concept a concrete example and explain its purpose, rather than just defining the term.
Key concepts in this lesson

The 2022 course is organised around nine key concepts. This lesson connects most directly to **change** — models exist to predict how outcomes change when one variable changes, ceteris paribus — and to **efficiency** in reasoning, since a good model achieves understanding with the fewest necessary assumptions. Being able to link a key concept to real-world material is specifically rewarded in the internal assessment, so practise naming the concept a piece of analysis illustrates. This lesson is identical at SL and HL; HL extension content begins in Unit 2.
Where this leads

Everything that follows in the course is model-building. Demand and supply, elasticity, market failure and macro policy are all simplified models resting on assumptions and analysed ceteris paribus, and every policy question invites both positive analysis (what will happen) and normative judgement (whether it should happen). The methodological habits you build here — isolate one variable, test your claims, keep facts and values apart, and stay alert to behavioural limits — are what turn a list of diagrams into genuine economic thinking.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 (a) answer marked: explain positive vs normative statements and the role of ceteris paribus
